rows.v: Hello / Good morning
bohn-ZHOOR
Bonjour, madame. — Hello, ma'am.
An interjection — invariable. Literally 'good day' (bon + jour).
The default polite greeting for daytime. In France, saying 'bonjour' when entering a shop is expected etiquette.
Good evening
bohn-SWAHR
Bonsoir à tous. — Good evening, everyone.
Literally 'good evening' (bon + soir). Invariable.
Switch from 'bonjour' to 'bonsoir' in the late afternoon/evening. For going to bed, use 'bonne nuit'.
Hi / Bye (casual)
sah-LU
Salut, ça va ? — Hi, how's it going?
Informal interjection. The final 't' is silent.
Works as both 'hi' and 'bye' among friends. Never use it in formal or professional settings.
Goodbye
oh ruh-VWAHR
Au revoir, à demain. — Goodbye, see you tomorrow.
Literally 'until seeing (again)'. A fixed phrase.
The standard polite goodbye. The 'r' is the soft French throaty 'r' that takes practice.
See you soon
ah byan-TOH
À bientôt ! — See you soon!
'À' (until) + 'bientôt' (soon). The final 't' is silent.
Warmer than 'au revoir' when you expect to see someone again shortly. 'À plus' is the casual version.
How are you? (formal)
koh-mahn tah-lay VOO
Comment allez-vous ? — How are you?
Formal/plural 'vous' form, using the verb 'aller' (to go) — literally 'how do you go?'.
French asks 'how do you go?' rather than 'how are you?'. The informal version is 'comment vas-tu ?'.
How's it going? / OK
sah VAH
— Ça va ? — Ça va ! — How's it going? — Good!
From 'aller'. 'Ça va ?' as a question and 'ça va' as the answer — same words.
Wonderfully economical: '— Ça va? — Ça va.' is a complete exchange meaning '— You good? — I'm good.'
Nice to meet you
ahn-shahn-TAY
Enchanté ! — Pleased to meet you!
Literally 'enchanted'. Add an extra 'e' in writing if you're female: 'enchantée' (sound is identical).
Said when first introduced. The spelling changes for gender ('enchanté'/'enchantée') though it sounds the same.
What's your name? (formal)
koh-mahn voo zah-play VOO
Comment vous appelez-vous ? — What's your name?
Formal form of reflexive 's'appeler' — literally 'how do you call yourself?'. Informal: 'comment tu t'appelles ?'.
Answer with 'Je m'appelle…' (My name is…). The double 'l' and reflexive structure take practice.
Good night
bun NWEE
Bonne nuit, dors bien. — Good night, sleep well.
Feminine 'bonne' agrees with feminine 'nuit'. Only for going to sleep, not as an evening greeting.
Reserved for bedtime. Don't use it to greet someone in the evening — that's 'bonsoir'.
Yes
WEE
Oui, merci. — Yes, thank you.
Invariable. The casual 'ouais' (yeah) is the spoken-French equivalent of 'yep'.
One of the first words everyone knows. 'Si' is used for 'yes' only to contradict a negative question.
No
NOHN
Non, merci. — No, thank you.
Invariable. The 'n' is nasalized, not fully pronounced.
To negate a verb, French wraps it: 'ne … pas' ('je ne parle pas' = I don't speak).
Please (formal)
seel voo PLEH
Un café, s'il vous plaît. — A coffee, please.
Literally 'if it pleases you'. Formal/plural; informal is 's'il te plaît'.
Often abbreviated 'SVP' in writing. Essential politeness — French places high value on it.
Thank you
mehr-SEE
Merci beaucoup. — Thank you very much.
Invariable. Strengthen with 'merci beaucoup' (thank you very much).
To say 'no thank you', 'non merci'. Beware: 'merci' alone can sometimes mean a polite 'no' when offered food.
You're welcome
duh RYAN
— Merci. — De rien. — Thanks. — You're welcome.
Literally 'of nothing'. A fixed reply.
The everyday response to 'merci'. More formal alternatives are 'je vous en prie' and 'avec plaisir'.
Sorry / Excuse me
par-DOHN
Pardon, je suis en retard. — Sorry, I'm late.
An interjection. Works to apologize or to get attention.
Use it to squeeze past someone or to apologize for a small mistake. Also said as a question to mean 'what did you say?'.
Excuse me
ehk-sku-zay MWAH
Excusez-moi, où est la gare ? — Excuse me, where's the station?
Formal/plural imperative of 's'excuser'. Informal: 'excuse-moi'.
The polite way to begin a question to a stranger. Slightly more formal than 'pardon'.
I don't understand
zhuh kohn-prahn PAH
Désolé, je ne comprends pas. — Sorry, I don't understand.
From 'comprendre'. Full form 'je ne comprends pas'; in speech the 'ne' is often dropped.
In casual spoken French the 'ne' commonly disappears: 'je comprends pas'. A key survival phrase.
Do you speak English?
par-lay voo zahn-GLEH
Parlez-vous anglais ? — Do you speak English?
Formal 'vous' form of 'parler'. Note 'anglais' is lowercase.
French doesn't capitalize languages. The liaison links 'vous' and 'anglais' as 'voo-zahn-gleh'.
One / A
UHN
Un café, s'il vous plaît. — One coffee, please.
Also the masculine indefinite article 'a/an'. Feminine is 'une'.
'Un' (masc.) and 'une' (fem.) double as both 'one' and 'a/an', agreeing with the noun's gender.
Two
DUH
Deux billets. — Two tickets.
Invariable. The 'x' is silent unless followed by a vowel (liaison).
In 'deux euros' the silent 'x' links as a 'z' sound: 'duh-zuh-roh'. Liaison is everywhere in French.
Three
TRWAH
Il est trois heures. — It's three o'clock.
Invariable. The 's' is silent except in liaison ('trois ans' → 'trwah-zahn').
Used for counting and time. 'Trois heures' = three o'clock, with liaison: 'trwah-zur'.
Ten
DEESS
J'ai dix euros. — I have ten euros.
Pronounced 'deess' alone, but 'dee' before a consonant and 'deez' before a vowel.
A shape-shifter in speech: 'dix' (deess), 'dix livres' (dee), 'dix ans' (deez). French numbers flex with liaison.
One hundred
SAHN
Cent euros. — A hundred euros.
Invariable for exactly 100, but takes an 's' in round multiples ('deux cents').
Tricky rule: 'cent' stays singular alone and in 'cent un', but adds 's' in 'deux cents', 'trois cents'.
What time is it?
kell UHR eh-TEEL
Quelle heure est-il ? — What time is it?
'Quelle' (which/what, fem.) agrees with feminine 'heure'. 'Est-il' inverts for the question.
Answer 'Il est trois heures' (It's three o'clock). French uses the impersonal 'il est' for time.
Today
oh-zhoor-DWEE
Aujourd'hui, il fait beau. — Today, the weather's nice.
An adverb of time — invariable. A long word built from older words meaning 'on the day of today'.
Pairs with 'hier' (yesterday) and 'demain' (tomorrow). The spelling is notoriously tricky even for natives.
Tomorrow
duh-MAN
À demain ! — See you tomorrow!
Adverb of time. The 'ain' ending is nasalized.
'À demain' (see you tomorrow) is a common goodbye. 'Après-demain' = the day after tomorrow.
Now
man-tuh-NAHN
Je dois partir maintenant. — I have to leave now.
Adverb. Two nasal sounds make it tricky: 'man-tuh-nahn'.
One of the most useful time words. 'Tout de suite' means 'right away / immediately'.
Water
OH
Une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît. — A jug of water, please.
Feminine: 'l'eau'. Pronounced simply 'oh' — three letters, one sound.
In French restaurants, ask for 'une carafe d'eau' to get free tap water rather than paying for bottled.
Coffee
kah-FAY
Un café, s'il vous plaît. — A coffee, please.
Masculine: 'le café'. Also means 'café' (the place).
Ordering 'un café' gets you an espresso. For coffee with milk, ask for 'un café crème' or 'un café au lait'.
Bread
PAN
Du pain, s'il vous plaît. — Some bread, please.
Masculine: 'le pain'. The 'ain' is nasal — don't pronounce the 'n' fully.
'Une baguette' is the classic loaf. False friend: 'pain' looks like English 'pain' but means bread.
The bill / check
ah-dee-SYOHN
L'addition, s'il vous plaît. — The check, please.
Feminine: 'l'addition'. Literally 'the addition' (the sum of the bill).
In France the bill won't arrive until you ask. Catch the server's eye and say 'l'addition, s'il vous plaît'.
Wine
VAN
Un verre de vin rouge. — A glass of red wine.
Masculine: 'le vin'. Nasal 'an' sound; the 'n' isn't fully pronounced.
'Vin rouge' (red), 'vin blanc' (white), 'vin rosé' (rosé). Wine is central to French dining culture.
Meal
ruh-PAH
Le repas était délicieux. — The meal was delicious.
Masculine: 'le repas'. The final 's' is silent; singular and plural look identical.
The three meals: 'le petit-déjeuner' (breakfast), 'le déjeuner' (lunch), 'le dîner' (dinner).
I would like
zhuh voo-DREH
Je voudrais un café. — I'd like a coffee.
The polite conditional of 'vouloir' (to want). Softer than 'je veux' (I want).
The courteous way to order. 'Je voudrais…' is gentler and more polite than the blunt 'je veux…'.
The menu
lah KART
La carte, s'il vous plaît. — The menu, please.
Feminine: 'la carte'. Note: 'le menu' often means a fixed-price set meal.
Order 'à la carte' to choose individual dishes; 'le menu' usually gets you a set multi-course deal.
Cheers!
sahn-TAY
Santé ! — Cheers!
Literally 'health'. A noun used as a toast. The final 'é' is a clear 'ay' sound.
The standard toast. 'À votre santé' (to your health) is the fuller, more formal version.
Where is…?
oo EH
Où est la gare ? — Where is the station?
'Où' (where, with accent) vs 'ou' (or). Uses 'est' (is) from 'être'.
The crucial travel question. The accent on 'où' distinguishes 'where' from 'or' (ou).
Toilet / Restroom
twah-LET
Où sont les toilettes ? — Where are the toilets?
Feminine and always plural: 'les toilettes'. Takes the plural verb 'sont' (are).
Always plural in French, so ask 'où SONT les toilettes ?'. 'Les WC' (vay-say) is also common.
Left
GOHSH
Tournez à gauche. — Turn left.
Feminine noun used in 'à gauche' (to the left). Also means 'clumsy' or, in politics, 'left-wing'.
Direction basics: 'à gauche' (left), 'à droite' (right), 'tout droit' (straight ahead).
Right
DRWAHT
C'est à droite. — It's on the right.
Feminine: 'la droite'. Don't confuse with 'tout droit' (straight ahead).
Classic trap: 'à droite' = to the right, but 'tout droit' = straight ahead. Listen for the difference.
Here
ee-SEE
Je suis ici. — I'm here.
Adverb of place. Pairs with 'là' (there).
'Ici' (here) vs 'là' (there). In speech, 'là' is often used loosely for both.
Over there
lah-BAH
C'est là-bas. — It's over there.
'Là' (there) + 'bas' (low/down) — literally 'there-below', meaning a more distant 'over there'.
Use 'là-bas' to point out something at a distance, often with a gesture.
Train station
GAR
La gare est près d'ici. — The station is near here.
Feminine: 'la gare'. Specifically a train station; an airport is 'l'aéroport'.
'La gare' is for trains; bus station is 'la gare routière'; subway/metro stop is 'la station'.
How much?
kohn-BYAN
Combien ça coûte ? — How much does it cost?
An adverb of quantity. 'Combien ça coûte ?' is the full everyday question.
Also 'combien de…?' for 'how many…?' ('combien de billets ?' = how many tickets?).
I
ZHUH
Je suis étudiant. — I am a student.
Subject pronoun. Shortens to 'j'' before a vowel ('j'ai' = I have).
Unlike Spanish, French keeps subject pronouns — you can't drop 'je'. It elides to 'j'' before vowels.
You (informal)
TU
Tu parles français ? — Do you speak French?
Informal singular 'you'. The 'u' sound (lips rounded, tongue forward) has no English equivalent.
Use 'tu' with friends, family, and peers. Use 'vous' for strangers, elders, and formal situations.
He / She
EEL / EL
Il est grand. Elle est grande. — He is tall. She is tall.
'Il' (he), 'elle' (she). Adjectives agree: 'grand' (m.) / 'grande' (f.).
'Il' and 'elle' also mean 'it', matching the gender of the noun. Adjective endings shift for gender.
We
NOO
Nous sommes amis. — We are friends.
Subject pronoun 'we'. In casual speech, 'on' often replaces 'nous' ('on est amis').
Spoken French very commonly uses 'on' (one/we) instead of 'nous' — listen for it everywhere.
Friend
ah-MEE
C'est mon meilleur ami. — He's my best friend.
'l'ami' (male), 'l'amie' (female) — same sound, different spelling. 'Un ami' / 'une amie'.
Gender shows in spelling, not sound. Note 'un(e) petit(e) ami(e)' means boyfriend/girlfriend, not just a friend.
Family
fah-MEE-yuh
Ma famille est grande. — My family is big.
Feminine: 'la famille'. The 'll' here sounds like 'y'. Singular, like English.
The 'ille' ending often sounds like 'ee-yuh'. A collective noun taking a singular verb.
Mother
MEHR
Ma mère s'appelle Marie. — My mother's name is Marie.
Feminine: 'la mère'. Note the accent: 'mère' (mother) vs 'mer' (sea) vs 'maire' (mayor) — all sound alike.
A famous trio of homophones: mère/mer/maire all sound 'mehr'. Affectionate: 'maman' (mom).
Father
PEHR
Mon père travaille beaucoup. — My father works a lot.
Masculine: 'le père'. 'Les parents' means 'the parents' (both) — also 'relatives'.
'Les parents' covers both parents together, and can also mean relatives in general. Affectionate: 'papa'.
Man / Woman
UM / FAM
L'homme et la femme. — The man and the woman.
'l'homme' (the 'h' is silent), 'la femme' (pronounced 'fam', irregularly). Both very common nouns.
'Femme' breaks the rules — it's pronounced 'fam', not 'fem'. The silent 'h' in 'homme' allows 'l'homme'.
To be
EH-truh
Je suis fatigué. — I am tired.
Highly irregular: je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils sont. The most essential verb.
French has one 'to be' (unlike Spanish's two). 'Être' is also a helper verb for many past tenses.
To have
ah-VWAHR
J'ai deux frères. — I have two brothers.
Highly irregular: j'ai, tu as, il a, nous avons, vous avez, ils ont. Also the main past-tense helper.
Used like Spanish 'tener' for age and hunger: 'j'ai faim' (I'm hungry), 'j'ai 20 ans' (I'm 20).
To go
ah-LAY
Je vais à Paris. — I'm going to Paris.
Irregular: je vais, tu vas, il va, nous allons, vous allez, ils vont. 'Aller' + infinitive = near future.
'Je vais manger' = 'I'm going to eat' — the simplest way to express the future. Also used in 'comment allez-vous ?'.
To do / make
FEHR
Qu'est-ce que tu fais ? — What are you doing?
Irregular: je fais, tu fais, il fait, nous faisons, vous faites, ils font. Covers 'do' and 'make'.
Also drives weather: 'il fait beau' (it's nice out), 'il fait froid' (it's cold). One verb, huge range.
To speak
par-LAY
Je parle un peu français. — I speak a little French.
Regular '-er' verb — the model for the largest verb group. je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons…
Master 'parler' and you've got the pattern for most French verbs, which end in '-er'.
To eat
mahn-ZHAY
On va manger ? — Shall we eat?
Mostly regular '-er', with a spelling tweak in 'nous mangeons' (keeping the soft 'g').
A near-model '-er' verb. The 'e' in 'mangeons' keeps the 'g' soft — a small but consistent spelling rule.
To want
voo-LWAHR
Je veux un café. — I want a coffee.
Irregular: je veux, tu veux, il veut, nous voulons, vous voulez, ils veulent.
'Je veux' (I want) is direct; 'je voudrais' (I'd like) is the polite form you'll use when ordering.
Can / to be able to
poo-VWAHR
Tu peux m'aider ? — Can you help me?
Irregular: je peux, tu peux, il peut, nous pouvons, vous pouvez, ils peuvent. Followed by an infinitive.
Essential for requests: 'pouvez-vous…?' (formal) / 'tu peux…?' (informal) = 'can you…?'.
To know (facts)
sah-VWAHR
Je sais. — I know.
Irregular: je sais, tu sais, il sait, nous savons, vous savez, ils savent. For facts and know-how.
French splits 'to know': 'savoir' (facts/skills) vs 'connaître' (people/places). 'Je sais' = I know (a fact).
No problem
pah duh proh-BLEM
— Merci. — Pas de problème. — Thanks. — No problem.
Literally 'not of problem'. A fixed casual phrase.
A relaxed reply to thanks or apologies. 'Aucun problème' (no problem at all) is slightly stronger.
OK / Agreed
dah-KOR
— On y va ? — D'accord. — Shall we go? — OK.
From 'être d'accord' (to be in agreement). 'D'accord' alone = OK / agreed.
The everyday 'OK'. 'Je suis d'accord' means 'I agree'. Extremely common in conversation.
Of course
byan SUR
— Tu viens ? — Bien sûr ! — Are you coming? — Of course!
Literally 'well sure'. An adverbial phrase, invariable.
An emphatic 'of course!'. Note the circumflex accent on 'sûr' (sure) vs 'sur' (on).
I'm hungry
zhay FAN
J'ai faim, on mange ? — I'm hungry, shall we eat?
Uses 'avoir' (to have) + 'faim' (hunger): literally 'I have hunger'. 'Faim' is nasal: 'fan'.
Like Spanish, French 'has' hunger rather than 'being' hungry. Similarly 'j'ai soif' = I'm thirsty.
I'm lost
zhuh swee pehr-DU
Pardon, je suis perdu. — Sorry, I'm lost.
Uses 'être' + 'perdu(e)'. Women write 'perdue' (sound is identical).
A vital travel phrase. The past participle 'perdu/perdue' agrees with the speaker's gender in writing.
I like / I love
ZHEM
J'aime le café. — I like coffee.
From 'aimer'. 'J'aime' = I like; for people it can mean 'I love'. 'J'adore' is stronger ('I love it').
Context decides intensity: 'j'aime' a thing = I like it; 'je t'aime' a person = I love you.
Enjoy your meal
bohn ah-pay-TEE
Bon appétit ! — Enjoy your meal!
Literally 'good appetite'. A fixed phrase said before eating.
Universally said before a meal in France, even to strangers nearby. The final 't' is silent.
How much is it?
say kohn-BYAN
C'est combien ? — How much is it?
Literally 'it's how much?'. The casual everyday way to ask a price.
'C'est combien ?' is the relaxed spoken form; 'combien ça coûte ?' is slightly more complete.
